                                                       Note on Title Change
                                    Effective with the current issue, the title of this publication
                                    has been shortened from Employment and Earnings and
                                    Monthly Report on the Labor Force to Employment and
                                    Earnings, the title used prior to February 1966, when it
                                    was merged with a former BLS publication, Monthly Report
                                    on the Labor Force. The content of this report remains
                                    unchanged. Household and establishment data will be shown
                                    in the identical or greater detail, and the customary analysis
                                    of employment and unemployment developments and special
                                    articles will also be continued.




Digitized for FRASER
http://fraser.stlouisfed.org/
Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis

                                    BLS Establishment Employment Estimates
                                    Revised to March 1968 Benchmark Levels
                                                                               *by Carol Utter
           The Bureau of Labor Statistics once again          The estimates for State and local govern-
       has adjusted its establishment payroll series      ments differed from their benchmarks by 3.6
       on employment, hours, earnings, and labor          percent. These estimates had been projected
       turnover to reflect the most recent complete       from benchmarks derived from the 1962 Census
       employment counts available for March 1968.        of Governments, whereas the March 1968 bench-
       These adjustments, which affect most of the        marks are derived from the October 1967 Census
       published series, generally mean that the em-      of Governments. The long interval b e t w e e n
       ployment series have been revised back to          benchmarks—5 years instead of the 1 year inter-
       March 1967 (the previous complete count) and       val at which benchmarks can be constructed for
       forward to the current month's estimate. The       other sectors—contributed to the relatively large
       series on State and local government employ-       difference between the estimates and bench-
       ment, on the other hand, have been adjusted        marks, particularly in the local education series.
       to levels shown by the October 1967 Census of      Therefore, revisions were carried back to 1964
       Governments and revised back to October 1964.      instead of to 1967 as for the other series, al-
       Estimates of hours and earnings and labor          though the adjustments to the figures in 1965
       turnover, which are weighted by employment         and 1964 are very small. The FederalGovern-
       estimates, also may be revised as a result of      ment employment figures are not revised, be-
       the changes in employment levels.                  cause the monthly data are total counts prepared
                                                          by the U.S. Civil Service Commission.
           The March 1968 total benchmark count of
       66.5 million workers on nonagricultural estab-         The March 1968 benchmarks were tabulated
       lishment payrolls was 0.4 percent or 238,000       according to the 1967 Standard Industrial Clas-

       ments were quite moderate for all major divi-      marks were tabulated on the 1957 SIC as amended
       sions except for State and local governments.      by the 1963 Supplement. This change caused
       (See table 1.) The latter was revised downward     several shifts of employees among industries in
       by 330,000 employees due to the introduction of    the trade and services divisions resulting in

       become available only at 5-year intervals.         ufacturing, two new 4-digit industries were

                                                                         SEASONAL FACTORS
                The following tables present seasonal adjustment factors for all series in the establishment section of this periodical,
        which have been revised, as in the past, coincidental with the adjustment of the industry employment series to new bench-
        marks. These factors will be revised at the time the industry employment statistics are again adjusted to later benchmarks
        and more current data are available. The seasonal movements are measured in order to adjust the data statistically for
        such recurring events as warm and cold weather, crop-growing cycles, holidays, vacations, regular industry model change-
        over periods, and the like. These movements are generally the largest single component of month-to-month changes in
        employment, hours, and labor turnover. The seasonal factors which follow enable the analyst to remove these influences
        from the data in order to determine more basic trends.
                These factors are to be used with data adjusted to the March 1968 benchmark.
             1:    Seasonal adjustment factors for employees on nonagricultural payrolls, by industry division and groups

                                                      Technical Note

                                          Additional information concerning the preparation
                                          of the labor force, employment, hours and earnings,
                                          and labor turnover series—concepts and scope,
                                          survey methods, and limitations—is contained in
                                          technical notes for each of these series, available
                                          from the Bureau of Labor Statistics free of charge.


       INTRODUCTION                                                        Relation between the household and payroll series


           The statistics in this periodical are compiled from                 The household and payroll data supplement one
       three major sources: (1) household interviews, (2) pay-             another, each providing significant types of information
       roll reports from employers, and (3) administrative                 that the other cannot suitably supply. Population charac-
       statistics of unemployment insurance systems,,                      teristics, for example, are readily obtained only from
                                                                           the household survey whereas detailed industrial classi-
           Data based on household interviews are obtained from            fications can be reliably derived only from establish-
       a sample survey of the population 16 years of age and               ment reports.
       over. The survey is conducted each month by the Bureau
       of the Census for the Bureau <of Labor Statistics and pro-              Data from these two sources differ from each other
       vides comprehensive data on the labor force, the em-                because of differences in definition and coverage, sources
       ployed and the unemployed, including such characteristics           of information, methods of collection, and estimating
       as age, sex, color, marital status, occupations, hours of           procedures. Sampling variability and response errors
       work, and duration of unemployment. The survey also                 are additional reasons for discrepancies. The major
       provides data on the characteristics and past work ex-              factors which have a differential effect on levels and
       perience of those not in the labor force. The information           trends of the two series are as follows:
       is collected by trained interviewers from a sample of
       about 50,000' households, representing 449 areas in 863                Employment

       Benchmark Adjustments
                                                                            THE SAMPLE
           Employment estimates are compared periodically
       with comprehensive counts of employment which provide                Design
       "benchmarks" for the various nonagriculturalindustries,              The sampling plan used in the current employment
       and appropriate adjustments are made as indicated. The           statistics program is known as "sampling proportionate
       industry estimates are currently projected from March            to average size of establishment." This design is an
       1967 levels. Normally, benchmark adjustments are made            optimum allocation design among strata since the sam-
       annually.                                                        pling variance is proportional to the average size of
                                                                        establishments. The universe of establishments is strati-
           The primary sources of benchmark information are             fied first by industry and then within each industry by
       employment data, by industry, compiled quarterly by              size of establishment in terms of employment. For each
       State agencies from reports of establishments covered            industry, the number of sample units is distributed
       under State unemployment insurance laws. These tabu-             among the size class cells on the basis of average em-
       lations, covering three-fourths of the total nonagricultural     ployment per establishment in each cell. In practice, this
       employment in the United States, are prepared under the          is equivalent to distributing the predetermined total num-
       direction of the Manpower Administration. Benchmark              ber of establishments required in the sample among the
       data for the residual are obtained from the records of the       cells on the basis of the ratio of employment in each cell
       Social Security Administration, the Interstate Commerce          to total employment in the industry. Within each non-
       Commission, and a number of other agencies in private            certainty stratum the sample members are selected at
       industry or government.                                          random.

                                                            ATTENTION
             As discussed in the Technical Note, the Bureau periodically adjusts the industry employment series to a recent
       benchmark to improve their accuracy. These adjustments may also affect the hours and earnings series because employ-
       ment levels are used as weights. Industry data for all national series shownin this report have been adjusted to March
       1968 benchmarks. Data from April 1968 forward are subject to revision at the time of the next benchmark.

            Beginning with the July 1969 and subsequent issues of Employment and Earnings, the national data in sections B,
       C, andD supersede those published in previous issues, as well as those appearing in the Handbook of Labor Statistics,
             Comparable data will be published in Employment and Earnings, United States, 190 9-69, BLS Bulletin 1312-7.
